attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a chief complaint of progressively worsening fatigue and shortness of breath on exertion over the past month. Her medical history reveals a previous diagnosis of myocardial infarction five years ago for which she is taking atorvastatin, aspirin and valsartan. She also has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections, for which she takes trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, and aortic stenosis for which she had undergone aortic valve replacement one year ago. She is a smoker, with a history of 2-pack-years, and does not drink alcohol. On examination her blood pressure is 145/87 mmHg, pulse 95/min and temperature 37.2oC. A yellowish tinge to her sclera is noted and a high-pitched diastolic murmur is heard best at the left sternal border. Abdominal examination is unremarkable. Her laboratory investigation results are as follows:

  • Hb: 95 g/L (130-180)

  • MCV: 87 fL (80-96)

  • Platelet count: 234 x10^9/L (150-400)

  • Lactate dehydrogenase: 273 U/L (135-225)

  • Haptoglobin: Undetectable (0.3-2.0)

  • Total bilirubin: 35 µmol/L (5-21)

Blood film review: Prominent anaemia with numerous schistocytes seen.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has presented with signs and symptoms of anaemia, which is confirmed by laboratory findings of decreased haemoglobin, the anaemia is most likely due to intravascular haemolysis because of the elevated bilirubin, low haptoglobin and elevated LDH. The diagnosis in this scenario is most likely macroangiopathic haemolytic anaemia because the patient has a history of aortic valve replacement, this along with the presence of schistocytes that demonstrates mechanical wear and tear of the RBCs along with the diastolic murmur indicates that the patient's haemolytic anaemia is due to her replaced heart valve.

Hereditary spherocytosis can present with signs and symptoms indicating haemolytic anaemia, but a peripheral smear in this condition would demonstrate spherocytes, and patients will usually have splenomegaly on abdominal examination.

G6PD deficiency can be a plausible diagnosis in this case, especially due to her history of use of trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, which can trigger haemolytic episodes in patients with G6PD deficiency, but it is not associated with the presence of schistocytes on peripheral smear, instead, there is usually the presence of Heinz bodies and bite cells on peripheral smear.

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is a rare disorder characterised by microvascular thrombosis and thrombocytopenia. It typically presents with a pentad of symptoms, including fever, neurological abnormalities, renal dysfunction, thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia. While the patient in the scenario does have some laboratory findings suggestive of microangiopathic haemolysis (elevated LDH, decreased haptoglobin and schistocytes), there are no other clinical features mentioned that would point towards TTP, and the patient's platelet count is also normal.

Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia is characterised by the destruction of red blood cells by autoantibodies. It can present with anaemia, elevated LDH, and indirect hyperbilirubinaemia, but in this case, the patient's history, physical examination findings, and laboratory results are not consistent with autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, it is also not associated with the presence of schistocytes on the peripheral blood smear. Spherocytes are the typical finding.
learningPoints: Macroangiopathic haemolytic anaemia is characterised by the presence of schistocytes on a peripheral blood smear.
references: Alkhouli M, Farooq A, Go RS, Balla S, Berzingi C. Cardiac prostheses-related hemolytic anemia. Clin Cardiol. 2019 Jul;42(7):692-700. doi: 10.1002/clc.23191. Epub 2019 May 6. PMID: 31039274; PMCID: PMC6605004.
